title: The year of wonder
paragraph: The future of journalism in 2026 begins with wonder. As AI accelerates and expands in media, journalism needs a pedagogy of wonder. This is an approach in education that encourages students to be critical, curious, and creative. For journalists, a pedagogy of wonder calls on them to become explorers, treating AI as a partner in inquiry to help them ask better questions, notice more, and deepen public understanding. It treats the newsroom as a learning space where curiosity is a method, and ethics is a practice. This isn’t about automating judgment but about expanding human insight.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: With this approach, AI technologies are not a flawed tool or a looming threat, but a partner in curiosity. Wonder invites journalists to ask not, “How can AI help me?” but “What new questions can I ask with AI by my side?” In reporting, this means leveraging AI tools to find and scan public data to reveal patterns, then validating them with on-the-ground reporting. It means using language models to map absent voices in a debate. It transforms AI from an efficiency tool into a prism for the imagination.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: Philosophically, the idea that learning begins in wonder has roots in ancient Greek thought. Journalists can take a cue from Socrates in using AI. For Socrates, wonder began with seeing human limits, recognizing how little is known and how much there is to learn, and accepting that yesterday’s answers may be inadequate. Following Plato’s example, journalists can spark wonder by testing familiar ideas about news and considering unfamiliar and perhaps uncomfortable alternatives.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: When journalists collaborate with AI in storytelling, creativity flourishes. Teams can prototype formats that were challenging to create before. AI becomes a sandbox for safe experimentation, testing, and redrawing the boundaries of genre and medium.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: Critical thinking and ethical reflection are heightened, not diminished. Wonder is not blind optimism, but it demands deeper questions. Journalists interrogate biases and systemic blind spots, using their innate curiosity to probe where algorithms might mislead or marginalize. They act as custodians of both technology and truth, responsible for upholding journalistic integrity.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: The lens of wonder helps journalism move past fear. Fear isn’t a friend; caution is. Caution means setting clear purposes, trying tools, and assessing results for the public good. It also means asking what should and shouldn’t be done with AI systems. The most dynamic news organizations will integrate these to advance inquiry and service, not to replace human judgment. They’ll use AI as an intellectual sparring partner to stress test ideas, explore alternatives, and surface gaps, transforming the newsroom into a laboratory of possibilities.

title: The year of wonder
paragraph: Media change isn’t new or linear. New media technologies, from TV to the web to generative AI, provoke optimism, anxiety, resistance, and acceptance. Change comes in stops and starts as the old and new dance together, coexisting and sometimes colliding. A pedagogy of wonder keeps journalism open to that dance. It values experiment and reflection. It invites collaboration across disciplines and communities. Journalists become explorers, channelling AI systems to help find their way. Let curiosity take the helm and let fear and anxiety recede.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: New Delhi: Trust has never been more fragile, nor more valuable. As generative AI fills feeds with flawless faces, scripted vulnerability, and manufactured moments that feel eerily human, consumers grow sharply aware of the difference between performance and truth. Even a small hint of insincerity can collapse a multimillion-dollar campaign, while brands that build genuine connection earn loyalty that algorithms cannot imitate.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: At the same time, the sophistication of AI-driven content has raised the bar for what “real” even looks like. In conversations with brands across sectors, one thing is increasingly clear: authenticity can no longer be treated as a tone of voice; it must be a system. Forward-thinking marketers are redesigning every touchpoint, messaging, experience, leadership communication, and internal culture to ensure that what they say aligns with what they do.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Today, trust has become the only competitive advantage that artificial intelligence cannot fake. And the brands that understand this are the ones building resilience that lasts beyond trends, tools, or algorithms.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: The widespread availability of generative artificial intelligence has triggered one of the most significant shifts in consumer psychology in decades. When flawless text, imagery, and video can be produced instantly, and at negligible cost, perfection no longer signals excellence; it signals automation. Consumers now instinctively associate effortless flawlessness with algorithmic origin, while reserving their deepest trust for content that bears visible traces of human time, thought, and effort.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Yet this heightened scepticism coexists with rising expectations of intelligent, technology-enabled support throughout the entire customer journey. Today’s consumers anticipate seamless, personalised experiences powered by AI, such as immersive virtual try-ons and augmented-reality previews, context-aware shopping guides, hyper-relevant product recommendations, and instant, intelligent chat responses that resolve queries in seconds. They welcome AI when it removes friction and delivers genuine value, but they demand absolute clarity about when and how it is being used.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Transparent labelling of AI-generated or AI-assisted content is imperative. Clear and unambiguous disclosure, whether through watermarks, captions or explicit statements, turns potential scepticism into informed consent and strengthens overall credibility.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Artificial intelligence should be used to enhance, not replace, genuine human connection. When AI supports the expression of sentiment, enables faster empathetic responses and allows people to focus on meaningful interactions, it serves as a multiplier of trust rather than a factor that undermines it.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Brands that treat these principles as foundational are the ones earning permission to operate at the intersection of cutting-edge technology and enduring human connection.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Certain characteristics now serve as near-instant red flags of generative or heavily automated origin. When several appear together, consumer trust collapses rapidly in the following ways:

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Leading organisations embed authenticity as a core discipline and use AI as a transparent enabler, never a substitute. Six proven practices set them apart:

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Mandate transparent disclosure: Label all AI-generated or AI-assisted content clearly with watermarks, captions, or explicit statements. Honest attribution builds credibility and turns technology into a trusted asset.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Share selective, insight-led transparency: Use AI analytics to pinpoint real challenges that matter to customers, then disclose them thoughtfully. Authentic friction, revealed at the right moment, becomes proof of integrity.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Amplify distinctive individual voices: Let named employees speak publicly in their own style. Provide AI drafting support only as a starting point, with strict human sign-off to preserve personality.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Safeguard a living institutional voice: Codify unique linguistic traits and values, then use AI merely to detect drift. Human irregularities must remain the hallmark of the brand.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Automate the mundane, humanise the meaningful: Deploy AI for routine tasks and logistics. Reserve personal video replies, live sessions, and handwritten notes for high-impact moments that shape loyalty.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Convert errors into trust accelerators: Pair real-time AI detection with swift, named human accountability. A candid admission, clear root cause, and immediate fix outperform any illusion of perfection.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: The most successful organisations do not reject artificial intelligence; they deploy it judiciously. Automation performs well when handling repetitive inquiries and logistical updates, allowing human talent to focus on deeper, higher-value interactions. The guiding principle is simple. Technology should remain invisible in moments that matter most to the customer relationship.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Every communication touchpoint should be assessed through one clear question. Does this interaction reflect the presence of people who genuinely care about the outcome, or does it resemble the output of a process attempting to imitate care?

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: Over the next decade, AI-generated content will dominate most digital interactions. Flawless images, impeccable explanations, and seamless apologies will fade into the endless scroll, just like background noise. In that world, the brands consumers trust will not be those with the cleverest prompts or the most advanced models. They will be the ones who deliberately leave traces of humanity.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: We see it today. A founder going live on a shaky phone to explain a delayed launch connects far more powerfully than any polished production. A support agent who spends four minutes writing an honest reply instead of four seconds regenerating one feels like a real person at the other end. A company that ships the wrong order, admits the mistake within minutes, and overnight-delivers the fix with a handwritten note earns stories, loyalty, and fierce defenders.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: A recent Gartner survey reveals that 64% of customers would prefer companies not to use AI for customer service, underscoring that nearly half of consumers now rank the lack of human interaction as their biggest frustration with automated systems. Conversely, brands that seamlessly combine AI efficiency with authentic personal engagement, such as Apple’s in-store Genius Bar teams, who provide calm and bespoke support, achieve markedly higher customer satisfaction and loyalty. In a similar vein, Emirates has risen to the forefront of global service rankings by prioritising empathetic human judgement over pure automation, demonstrating that genuine connection remains a powerful differentiator in an increasingly AI-driven landscape.

title: Authenticity in the age of AI: How brands can build lasting trust
paragraph: The sharpest teams are not rejecting AI. They are using it to remove drudgery so real people can show up where it counts. The winners will not outspend or out-engineer anyone. They will simply keep choosing the harder, slower, more human path, even when every incentive screams to fake it. That choice, increasingly rare and impossible to replicate at scale, is the last competitive advantage no algorithm can steal.

title: AI-Generated Content vs Human Creativity: Finding the Perfect Balance
paragraph: AI-Generated Content vs Human Creativity - The rise of AI tools has reshaped the way marketers plan, create, and publish content. Tasks that once required hours of research, drafting, and refinement can now be completed in minutes, but speed alone does not drive meaningful results. Today’s audiences seek originality, clarity, and trust qualities that only human creativity, judgment, and lived experience can provide.Here, it talks about what AI-generated content truly means for modern marketing, how it differs from human-crafted work, and where each holds unique strengths. It discusses the risks of relying solely on automation, the importance of authentic storytelling, and why emotional connection still drives engagement. Also, how audiences and search engines evaluate AI content, what signals matter most for credibility, and how brands can blend AI efficiency with human insight to stand out.Finally, it offers a practical roadmap for building a future ready content strategy that keeps your brand ahead with balance, quality, and purpose.AI generated content has changed modern marketing as it helps brands to create, manage, and share digital content faster and smarter. Artificial intelligence makes marketing work more efficient, scalable, and highly personalized using customer data and behavior. The benefits of using AI in modern marketing are that it increases the efficiency and speed, cost savings, enhances personalization, scalability, and gives data driven insights. Even marketers are using it today for their content creation, campaign optimization, translation and localization, and audience segmentation.AI tools in marketing have grown from simple data analysis to smart generative AI for creating content, images, and videos. Early tools analyzed data, mid tools have automated writing, and today’s AI works with humans for faster content, better personalization, and creative strategy, making marketing more efficient and scalable.AI generated content in marketing improves the scale, speed, and cost efficiency, but it sometimes lacks a deep emotional connection. Human created content builds trust, stronger engagement, and lasting relationships. The best marketing strategy uses both AI for fast production and humans for creativity, authenticity, and audience trust across all platforms and campaigns.The difference between machine written and human crafted content shapes how a brand is seen and trusted. Human written content drives authenticity, and readers connect more with it because of its genuine tone, and AI protects a brand’s reputation and strengthens its accountability.Human created content naturally carries experience and personal understanding , brings storytelling and empathy ,it also safeguards ethical standards that AI cannot imitate and it helps brands to maintain quality and long term SEO visibility, and lacks warmth and authenticity.In today’s market, success depends on how AI is balanced with smart efficiency and humans for emotional intelligence and creativity. Blending both creates credible, high-performing marketing built on trust and authenticity.AI handles the science of marketing speed, data, and automation, while humans bring art, emotion, and cultural understanding. AI improves efficiency, but human creativity builds real connections.Blending human creativity with AI efficiency builds marketing that is accurate, fast, and emotionally engaging, ensuring strong brand trust, ethical integrity, and long-term audience connection.Blending AI with human creativity is using AI as a smart helper for idea generation, research, and first drafts, as this speeds up the start of content creation while keeping human focus on creativity and strategy.AI gives quick ideas, titles and themes for campaigns as It can study search trends and competitor content to suggest related topics. It helps overcome creative blocks by offering fresh and endless ideas that inspire better stories.AI can summarise long reports and market studies into simple insights. It highlights the key data, facts and quotes. It also spots content gaps and competitor strengths to guide better marketing plans.AI creates well structured outlines and basic drafts for blogs, emails, or product descriptions in minutes.It is called human refinement which turns AI generated drafts into engaging, brand accurate content. This step focuses on adding emotion, insight and authenticity that AI cannot create on its own.AI produces text that sounds correct but it often misses the unique tone. Then the Human marketers can edit the draft to match the brand personality formal or friendly so it feels familiar and trustworthy to the audience.AI writes logically but human touch makes it feel real. Marketers add personal stories, relatable feelings and thoughtful expressions that create emotional connection and make readers feel understood.AI gathers existing facts but cannot share real experiences or original thoughts. Human experts include first hand knowledge, examples and observations that make content credible, valuable and search friendly.It focuses on using AI again to boost the reach and performance of human-refined content. At this stage, AI helps with SEO , technical setup and repurposing content for different digital platforms to gain maximum visibility.AI finds the best keywords, improves sentence flow, and checks how readable the content is. It suggests updates to boost search rankings and can create catchy titles and meta descriptions that attract more clicks online.AI handles behind-the-scenes details that help search engines understand the content better. It adds schema markup for better visibility, writes accurate image descriptions, and reduces file sizes to make web pages load faster.AI turns one long article into several short social posts, email summaries and scripts. It also changes content formats to suit different platforms, helping brands reach more audiences effortlessly.focuses on Quality Control and E-E-A-T, the final and most important stage of blending AI and human effort. It ensures the content is accurate and trustworthy.Humans review all facts, figures and claims to catch any AI mistakes. This step prevents the misinformation, updates outdated data and protects the brand’s credibility by ensuring every detail is correct and reliable.Editors make sure the content feels lived in by using practical examples and real situations. They highlight expert knowledge and the brand’s authority, helping readers and search engines see the content as knowledgeable and trustworthy.A human reviewer takes responsibility for ethics, fairness, and tone. The goal is to make sure the content sounds honest, balanced, and respectful, which builds lasting trust with audiences.Audiences trust human created content more than AI content. Search engines like Google focus on quality, originality and E-E-A-T - Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trustworthiness rather than the content’s source.Transparency about AI use is important but affects trust differently. Clear disclosure with human oversight maintains integrity. The most effective approach blends AI efficiency with human creativity to ensure authenticity, trust and long term performance.Google values people first content that is original, informative and clearly written by knowledgeable sources. E-E-A-T (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness) remains central.Google strictly penalizes AI generated “spam” content used to manipulate the rankings. Only content that informs or educates real user needs aligns with Google’s quality and spam policies.AI works best for handling large amounts of data, personalizing content and increasing speed. Also, it often fails when used for tasks that need human empathy, cultural understanding and careful ethical judgment.To keep content authentic while using AI , set clear rules for tone that matches the brand’s personality and ethics. Editors should add real experiences and feelings to make AI content more relatable and credible. Let AI handle large or repetitive tasks like product listings or ad variations. Rely on humans for sensitive topics and storytelling that need empathy and depth.The future of content creation will be a partnership between humans and AI. The New Emerging Roles are Prompt Strategist, AI Ethicist, Brand Storyteller , and Content Optimization Analyst . Modern marketers must balance creativity with technology. They need critical thinking to check AI accuracy, creativity to plan meaningful stories, and basic AI knowledge to use digital tools effectively.Responsible AI use in content marketing demands for ethical transparency to avoid any Bias and Respect Boundaries, data privacy, and strict quality control. Brands must combine AI efficiency with human judgment to maintain trust, accuracy, and originality. Regular bias checks, legal compliance, and fact verification ensure credible, user focused content that aligns with global standards and brand integrity.The rise of generative search and AI powered answer engines is changing content strategy. Success now depends less on keyword rankings and more on creating high quality, authoritative, and structured content that AI can understand, cite and include in summaries an approach known as Generative Engine Optimization GEO ).This focus on New Focus Areas as instead of chasing clicks, brands aim to get featured in AI generated responses. Instead of chasing clicks, brands aim to get featured in AI-generated responses.Use structured headings, short paragraphs, lists, and tables, schema markup to define entities and relationships, helping AI interpret the meaning and relevance of the content, and build E-E-A-T. Write in a natural tone that answers long, question-based searches. Add FAQ sections to cover related subtopics for broader reach. Generative Engine Optimization is a major shift in brands that combine human expertise with technical clarity and true usefulness will lead to AI-driven search visibility.AI has opened new creative frontiers for marketers, but its real strength shows when it works alongside human creativity rather than replacing it. As audiences increasingly look for originality, trust, and a genuine brand voice, it is clear that marketers must move beyond the automated outputs and focus on content that feels real, useful, and emotionally connected. Search engines are also shifting toward rewarding depth, clarity, and quality over sheer volume, making human judgment and strategic thinking more important than ever. The future will favour marketers who know how to combine both worlds using AI to scale smarter and faster while relying on human insight, storytelling, and intuition to create content that truly stands out and builds lasting trust.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: One viral video shows what appears to be four sharks swimming in a Jamaican hotel's pool as floodwaters allegedly brought on by Hurricane Melissa swamp the area. Another purportedly depicts Jamaica's Kingston airport completely ravaged by the storm. But neither of these events happened, it's just AI-generated misinformation circulating on social media as the storm churned across the Caribbean this week.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: Some of the clips appear to be spliced together or based on footage of old disasters. Others appear to be created entirely by AI video generators.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: “I am in so many WhatsApp groups and I see all of these videos coming. Many of them are fake,” said Jamaica’s Education Minister Dana Morris Dixon on Monday. “And so we urge you to please listen to the official channels.”

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: Although it's common for hoax photos, videos and misinformation to surface during natural disasters, they’re usually debunked quickly. But videos generated by new artificial intelligence tools have taken the problem to a new level by making it easy to create and spread realistic clips.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: In this case, the content has been showing up in social media feeds alongside genuine footage shot by local residents and news organizations, sowing confusion among social media users.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: Look for a watermark logo indicating that the video was generated by Sora, a text-to-video tool launched by ChatGPT-maker OpenAI, or other AI video generators. These will usually appear in one of the corners of a video or photo.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: It is quite easy to remove these logos using third-party tools, so you can also check for blurs, pixelation or discoloration where a watermark should be.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: Look more closely at videos for unclear details. While the sharks-in-pool video appears realistic at first glance, it looks less believable upon closer examination because one of the sharks has a strange shape.

title: Phony AI-Generated Videos of Hurricane Melissa Flood Social Media Sites
paragraph: You might see objects that blend together, or details such as lettering on a sign that are garbled, which are telltale signs of AI-generated imagery. Branding is also something to look out for as many platforms are cautious about reproducing specific company logos.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister Flags Deepfakes, Fake News, Misinformation And Social Media
paragraph: The Minister emphasized that the Government of India has proactively addressed the challenges arising from the ubiquitous nature of the internet, addressing issues such as deepfakes, misinformation, and disinformation.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister Flags Deepfakes, Fake News, Misinformation And Social Media
paragraph: Misuse Of Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister of State for Skill Development & Entrepreneurship, Electronics & IT and Jal Shakti Rajeev Chandrasekhar on Saturday participated in a fireside chat organized by MoneyControl in Bengaluru, sharing insights on Artificial Intelligence (AI) and its potential impact on India. He highlighted that AI can propel India’s digital economy, fostering growth in sectors such as healthcare, agriculture, and governance. He emphasized the positive influence AI could have on startups, expanding their opportunities and contributing to India’s overall development.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister Flags Deepfakes, Fake News, Misinformation And Social Media
paragraph: “We see AI as a significant bolt to the already galloping Indian digital economy, acting as a kinetic enabler. To enable the AI ecosystem, we have put together an overall framework that talks about AI compute capacity. The Government will deploy financial resources for building foundational models, Large Language Models (LLMs), and various use cases. Similar to the semiconductor model, we will fund startups as well. We maintain a clear focus on building an academic, industry, and startup research ecosystem, which we refer to as the Innovation and Research Center. There are many adjacent areas as well, including AI chips, where we will have an intersection between the semiconductor program and the India AI mission. AI compute will have two segments: one led by the private sector, similar to the design of the semiconductor ecosystem with incentivized investments. The other segment involves indigenously developed public sector capacity for AI emerging from C-DAC, which will be available to the Indian ecosystem,” said Rajeev Chandrasekhar.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister Flags Deepfakes, Fake News, Misinformation And Social Media
paragraph: He highlighted the challenge of the chip shortage, noting that it is expected to resolve relatively quickly and emphasized the need for India to shift its focus towards nurturing AI talent, anticipating a growing ecosystem in the future.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Union Minister Flags Deepfakes, Fake News, Misinformation And Social Media
paragraph: “We should focus on creating a global framework, not an American or European framework. The nature of the internet and our experiences on social media, the toxicity, and the crimes or harms it has shown us, that regardless of any country that has built rules and laws, the ubiquitous nature of the internet means that 80-90% of cybercrime or harms are extra-jurisdictional. The perpetrator may be in one jurisdiction, the victim in another, and the crimes in a third. Our approach is to ensure there are some principles, make sure there is a list of harms and criminalities as we see it today, and then keep adding to it as we encounter malicious models, biased, bigoted models, and algorithms. Deepfakes are a classic example because misinformation and patently false information are diseases that social media has spread, causing harm, especially in democratic countries. It creates divisions, incitements, and fake narratives. Misinformation has been a problem in social media; now imagine misinformation powered by AI,” the Minister further added.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: NEW DELHI: The Ministry of Information and Broadcasting is set to take a major technological leap in its fight against misinformation and fake news with the launch of a fact-checking chatbot.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: The Press Information Bureau (PIB) has already put in place an institutional mechanism as part of its Fact Check Unit (FCU) to ensure swift and coordinated responses to fake news and false claims.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: According to Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw, the Artificial Intelligence (AI) Chatbot will also provide details to a query about a subject or a claim, which had been fact-checked in the past.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: “We have got a fact-check chatbot made and tested. We will launch it after the final testing. As soon as you put a query, it will produce information, even if it has happened a year ago. The chatbot will also have a ‘search’ option,” said officials.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: The ministry is simultaneously developing separate software to check the veracity of a video, to counter the Deepfake menace. It is at a preliminary stage.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot
paragraph: “As soon as you put the video in that software, the related authentic video will appear… Many Deepfake videos about the Indian Army are being run by foreign people's handles, false claims are made while the original is about something else…To address this particular issue, we are working on software. It is to overcome the damage caused by the time taken to check the authenticity of a clip, fact check and put the correct information in the public domain because sometimes it takes a long time to fact check,” an official explained.

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot

title: Ministry of I&B to fight misinformation, fake news with fact-checking chatbot

To improve the quality of backgrounders, we have organised workshops for our officials. The workshops were conducted by senior journalists,” said Vaishnaw.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: New Delhi: India has extended an official invitation to China to participate in its weeklong 2026 AI Impact Summit in New Delhi, a top government official told reporters here on Monday. This marks the first time that India has extended an official invitation to China to participate in its annual artificial intelligence event as a partnering nation, as New Delhi seeks to showcase its foundational AI models and present a policy directive that other nations agree with.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: The move comes after China attended 2025’s AI Action Summit in France, where India was the co-chair nation—months after the country declined to be a signatory to the 2024 AI Summit in Seoul, South Korea.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: “We’ve invited numerous nations around the world, particularly with a focus on the global south, to participate in the AI Impact Summit—and have already received confirmations from many, including France, to have them as part of it. We have also sent a formal invitation to China to take part as well,” said S. Krishnan, secretary at the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (Meity).

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: China’s participation in global AI conversations has become a much-discussed factor, especially after China-origin DeepSeek and Qwen rose to the fore with cost-effective AI models that matched OpenAI’s Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) and Google’s Gemini.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: DeepSeek’s foundational models, released last year, promised to reduce the cost of training and operating AI models to a fraction of the current cost. However, their lack of global citations have not led to widespread adoption of their models at an enterprise level globally.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: Krishnan did not confirm if China will be one of the 100-plus nations in attendance in February. An email sent to the embassy of China did not receive a response until press time.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: The five-day India AI Impact Summit will take place from 15 to 20 February 2026. Krishnan said that over 50 heads of state are expected to be in attendance. Prime Minister Narendra Modi is expected to host a welcome dinner for heads of state and top tech executives in New Delhi on 18 February, Krishnan added.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: “We’ve also received confirmations from top executives from the AI world, including Bill Gates, Dario Amodei of Anthropic, Demis Hassabis of Google DeepMind, and more. Having large tech firms make key announcements will be an important part of the Summit, even if that wouldn’t be the main focus,” Krishnan added.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: India’s first AI summit in December 2023 saw a declaration signed on AI safety regulation by Global Partnership on AI (Gpai), a multinational body.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: “The idea would be to sign a definitive declaration, and the idea to sign a policy agreement that allows for a collaborative approach to regulating AI in a unified direction is also on the cards,” Krishnan said.

title: For the first time, India invites China to its AI Summit
paragraph: The secretary also added that there is ‘no conflict’ in terms of India’s approach to regulating AI, and no nation has raised ‘concerns’ about India’s approach to regulating AI.

title: YouTube to crack down on AI-generated and repetitive content: Google changes payout rules; check if you will be affected
paragraph: Repetitious uploads are so alike that a casual viewer gains no fresh value; think lightly cropped re-uploads or looping compilations. To pass review, a creator must add significant commentary, analysis, or creative storytelling, not just filters or speed changes.

title: YouTube to crack down on AI-generated and repetitive content: Google changes payout rules; check if you will be affected
paragraph: Scroll through YouTube today and you will run into the same pattern again and again: a synthetic voice gliding over stock video, a top-ten list clipped from a news site, or a slideshow padded with royalty-free loops. Advertisers complain, viewers bounce, and original creators watch their work sink beneath low-effort uploads. On 15 July 2025, YouTube will tighten its Partner Programme rules, cutting ad revenue for channels that push “mass-produced, repetitious, or inauthentic” content.The entry bar—1,000 subscribers plus either 4,000 public watch hours in 12 months or 10 million Shorts views in 90 days—stays the same, but it will no longer be enough on its own. YouTube says the update simply clarifies a long-standing requirement for “original and authentic” videos and gives reviewers better language for spotting spam and AI-generated slop. Creators who ignore the new line risk demonetisation even if their audience numbers look healthy.The support note explains that the platform must “better identify mass-produced and repetitious content” because automated tools now churn out near-identical clips at scale. This includes AI voice essays, templated slideshow channels, and endlessly recycled reaction footage that adds nothing new for viewers.Channels must still hit 1,000 subscribers and the watch-time or Shorts views goal before applying to the Partner Programme. Reviewers will then check recent uploads for authentic value. A channel that relies on reused footage with only minor tweaks can meet the numbers yet fail monetisation.YouTube is not banning AI tools; it is banning lazy AI usage. A synthetic narrator that adds genuine insight over fresh footage can still earn ads. An AI voice that reads a blog post on top of stock clips will likely fail. The same standard applies to templated slideshows or compilations that offer no added context.Separate guidance on YouTube’s help pages confirms that creators must be at least 16 years of age to stream alone. Younger teens can only appear if an adult host runs the broadcast. The rule fits YouTube’s broader push for safety, accountability, and protecting minors from online harassment, inappropriate content, live chat abuse, privacy violations, and potential exploitation during livestreams.They take effect on 15 July 2025 worldwide for all channels in the YouTube Partner Programme .Any video that looks factory-made or repeatedly reuses the same template without adding fresh commentary, education, or entertainment will fall under the new restriction. Typical examples include AI-generated listicles, looped compilations, and near-duplicate slideshow news.No. AI can still be used if the creator adds clear value, original insight, editing, or storytelling. Purely automated clips that read text over stock footage are likely to be demonetised.No. You still need 1,000 subscribers plus 4,000 public watch hours in the last 12 months or 10 million Shorts views in 90 days to apply. The review process, however, will weigh originality more heavily.YouTube’s primary penalty is demonetisation. Ads, Super Chat, and other revenue features shut off until you delete or significantly rework the problematic videos and reapply to YPP.Not necessarily. If the creator adds substantial commentary, transforms the clips, and provides real educational or entertainment value, the videos can still earn revenue. Simple re-uploads or minimal edits will not qualify.
