title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: While many U.S. workers fear that artificial intelligence is coming for their jobs, researchers say the rapid advancements of tools like ChatGPT and Claude haven't had a major impact on the overall labor market – at least not yet.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: The effectiveness of generative AI platforms to instantly write emails, analyze data and create travel plans can be wildly impressive, but a recent Remote Labor Index study that put these bots to the test showed just how far they still have to go to match the capabilities of humans.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Researchers tested how effective different company's AI models were at doing hundreds of freelance projects that people had been paid to do. Tasks included product design, architecture, data analysis and video animation.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: The top-performing system (Manus) successfully completing only 2.5% of the jobs, according to the study from Scale AI and the Center for AI Safety.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "Current models are not close to being able to automate real jobs in the economy," Jason Hausenloy, one of the researchers, told the Washington Post.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Despite these shortcomings, employers are scrambling to find ways to implement these tools and a Reuters/Ipsos poll from August found that nearly three-quarters of Americans believe AI will be "putting too many people out of work permanently."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: In Philadelphia, the City Council held a hearing in October on the potential government applications of AI. Officials said they hope to release guidelines this spring on how city employees can use the technology on their jobs.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "If anyone in the world would say that they were an AI expert, they would be lying, because it's continuously transforming. It's morphing into new technology," Melissa Scott, the chief information officer for the city's Office of Innovation and Technology, said at the hearing. "So you can be an expert on where AI is today, but three months from now it can be another way."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Trying to understand the future impact of artificial intelligence might come down to who you ask. While computer scientists tend to be maximalists, saying AI is going to change everything, most economists believe this is just the latest advancement in tech, and jobs will adapt just like they have with the invention of the steam engine, assembly line, computer, smartphones and everything in between.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: University of Pennsylvania professors Ioana Marinescu, an economist, and Konrad Kording, a computer scientist, sought to bring these two conflicting ideologies together in their research published in November. "We're trying to explain where these two camps are coming from to have such radically opposite conclusions" Marinescu said. "So we did theoretical work in economics to try to ask what assumptions are they making that are different."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: The professors developed an interactive model that split the economy into two fields: the intelligence sector and physical jobs, which includes construction workers, nurses and restaurant staff. The necessity of both and the fact that AI isn't really being deployed in the physical sector limits the technology's overall impact on the economy, they said.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "One of the key messages of our paper is that if you have complementarity – both the physical and the intelligence – then the impact of intelligence on growth and on wages is going to saturate, meaning there's only so far you can go," Marinescu said.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: If tech jobs become more automated because of the use of AI, it could cause a major shift from intelligence to physical jobs, but Marinescu said there hasn't been much evidence yet that this job migration is happening.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Looking at translators and customer service representatives can show the distinction between what AI is currently capable of doing and what it does well. While creating subtitles for TV shows and movies, for example, is a static task that can be done through automation, having AI handle online chats can prove problematic, even if it has become a common practice for many companies.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "The bot can do well in many of the more standard cases, but when you have a more outside-the-box case, businesses have found that the robots aren't very good at handling those," Marinescu said. "... It's not that easy to replace customer service because of these edge cases. Even though there's not many of them, it can end up eating the business out of money if you treat them the wrong way.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "I suspect that eventually they might figure it out, like maybe only get the real people to deal with those complicated cases. ... But let's put it this way, it's not so easy."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: As employers navigate how to implement this new technology into their industries, the ability to effectively use AI is becoming a highly sought-after attribute for hiring managers.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Locally, there were 10,815 job postings in the area that required AI skills in 2024, according to a July report from Brookings. The study ranked nearly 200 metro areas in the country based on their readiness to benefit from AI, and Philly came in at No. 14. The region was included in the second tier behind Silicon Valley and was given the designation of a "Star Hub" with "uniformly strong AI ecosystems, balancing top‑tier talent, research, and enterprise uptake."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: In some jobs, coexisting with these emerging technologies has proven challenging. Sorelle Friedler, a professor of computer science at Haverford College who served as the assistant director for data and democracy in the Biden administration, said one sector AI has been very disruptive is her own. She said she doesn't allow her students to use these platforms at all, unless it's a class on building or learning about AI.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "Part of the learning process is taking that time to struggle ... and I think that stands really opposed to what AI systems can do well and how these systems work because the goal of the system is to really efficiently get an outcome right," she said. "... Students need to learn all of these steps on their own."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: As for Marinescu's field of work, she said AI "can do OK" in some aspects of conducting and analyzing research, but it can't quite effectively complete an entire paper.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "That's one of the elements why I think we don't see a quicker effect (of AI)," she said. "Because it's one thing to say, 'Oh, in theory, could AI do your job?' Yes, maybe it can do part of it, but if you can't cover that last mile, it can still be hard to deploy."

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: Researchers aren't just interested in finding out if AI can replace humans in the workforce, but also how these platforms are impacting people who rely on them to complete mundane tasks.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: The Harvard Business Review published a study last year analyzing workers' production when they use AI on common duties, including crafting emails, writing social media posts and brainstorming. The study, which included more than 3,500 participants working with and without the tools, had mixed results.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: While researchers said AI enhanced some quality and efficiency, they warned that there were psychological costs, reporting an 11% drop in intrinsic motivation and 20% increase in boredom on average.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "I think that it can be really hard to know what your own efficiency is, let alone what a full company's efficiency is when using these tools without really careful measurement," said Friedler, who was not involved in the study.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: One example of an organization that is successfully implementing AI into its day-to-day operation is Veterans Affairs, Friedler said. The federal agency is starting to utilize the technology as a way to address its infamous backlog of disability claims.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: To determine if a veteran is eligible for benefits, VA staffers have to go through loads of paperwork that include service records, medical history and testimonies. There are hundreds of thousands of backlogged claims, meaning those veterans and their dependents are caught in bureaucratic limbo waiting to find out if and when they will receive payments.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: To help chip away at this stockpile, the VA has turned to AI. The agency said that "computer-based tasks, such as summarizing documents, routing information and responding to routine inquiries," will be automated within the next few years.

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: "Obviously, the VA is itself an important use case, but I also think that many of the other successful use cases have this same type of characterization," Friedler said. "Someone has looked and said, 'How can we sort of easily say yes to a lot of people for the things that we know we're going to approve anyway, and reserve human time for these more complex judgments?'"

title: AI tools fail at most real-world jobs, but workers still fear replacement
paragraph: AI is here to stay, and understanding its full potential might be the best way to prepare for its impact on the workforce.

title: What is Oracle’s generative AI strategy?
paragraph: While Microsoft, AWS, Google Cloud, and IBM have already released their generative AI offerings, rival Oracle has so far been largely quiet about its own strategy. Instead of launching a competing offering in a rush, the company is quietly preparing a three-tier approach.

title: What is Oracle’s generative AI strategy?
paragraph: “Our tier strategy resembles a three-layer cake and each of these layers targets different enterprise customers depending on their needs,” said Karan Batta, vice president of Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI).

title: What is Oracle’s generative AI strategy?
paragraph: The first tier, according to Batta, consists of its OCI Supercluster service and is targeted at enterprises, such as Cohere or Hugging Face, that are working on developing large language models to further support their customers.

title: Election Commission urges parties to disclose AI generated campaign content 'in interest of transparency'
paragraph: NEW DELHI: Warning against political campaigns that use Artificial Intelligence (AI) to create “synthetic” images, videos and audio, potentially influencing voter opinion and trust, the Election Commission on Thursday directed the political parties to prominently label such content as ‘AI generated’ or ‘synthetic’ while sharing it on social media, “in the interest of transparency and accountablity”.EC in an advisory sent to all recognised national and state parties said there was need for prominent and easily discernible labelling of AI-generated or synthetic content meant for political campaigning with the notation ‘AI-generated’, ‘digitally enhanced’ or ‘synthetic content’, to keep the voter informed. It added that campaign advertisements and promotional content disseminated online should include disclaimers wherever synthetic content is utilised.While referring to its guidelines dated May 6, 2024, on the ethical use of social media platforms in 2024 Lok Sabha election, EC on Thursday said they had sought to curb the possibility of parties and their representatives disturbing the level playing field with the use of ‘deep fakes’ and AI-generated distorted content to spread fake information, misinformation or disinformation. The purpose of the guidelines, it said, was to ensure strict avoidance of wrongful use of AI-generated content while the model code of conduct was in force.“However, now considering the deepening influence of AI generated/synthetic content in shaping public opinion, the Election Commission of India advises all political parties and their leaders, candidates and star campaigners to also take necessary measures for prominent labelling of AI-generated/synthetic content, if any, being shared for campaigning through their social media platforms and other media platforms,” the poll panel said on Thursday. It said prominent and easily discernible labelling of AI-generated content would ensure responsible and transparent campaigning as well as informed voters.“All political parties are urged to adhere to the advisory to ensure fair, transparent and responsible use of emerging technologies in election campaigning,” said EC.

title: Google Deepmind CEO says global AI cooperation 'difficult'
paragraph: Artificial intelligence pioneer and head of Google Deepmind's CEO Demis Hassabis on Monday said that greater international cooperation around AI regulation was needed but "difficult" to achieve "in today's geopolitical context".

title: Google Deepmind CEO says global AI cooperation 'difficult'
paragraph: At a time when AI is being integrated across all industries, its uses have raised major ethical questions, from the spread of misinformation to its impact on employment, or the loss of technological control.

title: Google Deepmind CEO says global AI cooperation 'difficult'
paragraph: At London's South by Southwest (SXSW) festival on Monday, Hassabis, who has won a Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his research on AI, also addressed the challenges that artificial general intelligence (AGI) -- a technology that could match and even surpass human capability -- would bring.

title: Google Deepmind CEO says global AI cooperation 'difficult'
paragraph: "The most important thing is it's got to be some form of international cooperation because the technology is across all borders. It's going to get applied to all countries," Hassabis said."Many, many countries are involved in researching or building data centres or hosting these technologies. So I think for anything to be meaningful, there has to be some sort of international cooperation or collaboration and unfortunately that's looking quite difficult in today's geopolitical context," he said.At Paris's AI summit in February, 58 countries -- including China, France, India, the European Union and the African Union Commission -- called for enhanced coordination on AI governance But the US warned against "excessive regulation", with US Vice President JD Vance saying it could "kill a transformative sector".Alongside the US, the UK refused to sign the summit's appeal for an "open", "inclusive" and "ethical" AI.Hassabis on Monday advocated for the implementation of "smart, adaptable regulation" because "it needs to kind of adapt to where the technology ends up going and what the problems end up being".

title: AI at a Crossroads: Global Experts Debate Ethics, Power & Access in Delhi
paragraph: From ethical AI and global regulation to chips, geopolitics and equitable access, global experts gather at Bharat Mandapam to debate whether artificial intelligence can be governed before it outpaces humanity. A deep dive into India's proposed Delhi Declaration and why the Global South matters in shaping AI's future.

title: TalentSprint Launches High-Impact Generative AI Program for Professionals
paragraph: Chennai: TalentSprint has announced an executive-friendly, four-month Generative AI Foundations and Applications program. title: TalentSprint Launches High-Impact Generative AI Program for Professionals

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: At Chicago Public Media, we are committed to producing journalism that is accurate, ethical, and deeply human. As generative artificial intelligence (GAI) tools like ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, and DALL-E become more available, this policy outlines how we use them responsibly, transparently, and in service of our mission. GAI is the term for artificial intelligence that generates new content like text, images, or video. GAI is trained on patterns of information in massive data sets to generate new outputs. While GAI may assist with certain tasks such as summarizing documents or analyzing data, our editorial and design content will be created and shaped by humans. This policy, which we expect to update frequently as we adapt to new technology, is intended to guide staff and inform the public about how we incorporate emerging technologies without compromising the trust our audience places in us.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Any significant use of GAI in content must be disclosed to the audience. Significant use includes any GAI-generated language appearing in final content without substantial rewriting by a human journalist (or, in the case of translations, thorough review by a human), or any GAI-based data analysis that has not been verified through non-GAI means. Significant GAI use in editorial content should only occur in rare use cases that serve an editorial purpose, only permitted after review by newsroom editors.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Idea generation/brainstorming; i.e. using AI for initial concept development, mood boards, or inspiration that leads to original human creation

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Using AI-generated visual elements as components within larger, human-created works (e.g., AI textures or non-specific visual elements in a designed infographic, AI backgrounds integrated into original photography)

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Incorporating AI-generated graphic assets that are substantially modified, edited, or transformed through human creative input

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Enhancing workflow efficiency through AI-assisted tasks like background removal, color correction, or basic photo editing

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Replicating or reproducing any journalist or actual person’s voice, image, or likeness, except to report on or exemplify AI capabilities

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Newsroom staff are responsible for adhering to the editorial process for accuracy in all content they create, regardless of whether GAI was used in the process

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: All GAI outputs must adhere to the same editorial and design standards, journalistic ethics, and accountability requirements as content produced without AI

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: When there is doubt about the accuracy or originality of AI-generated material, staff must not incorporate it into the editorial process

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Journalists are responsible for identifying and correcting any bias in GAI-generated content. GAI may amplify or reproduce biases in its training data and prompts; outputs must be held to the same ethical standards as human reporting.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Disclosure is always required when: A human voice is represented, such as digital text-to-audio conversions Any content has not been reviewed by a human, such as digital language translation tools GAI played a significant role in the creation of a story, beyond the uses permitted above.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: Staff must inform supervisors and editors and get approval for each individual instance of GAI use or for integrating GAI into editorial workflows and be prepared to explain how it was used and the extent of human modification applied in their process.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: If staff has issues or concerns about actual or suspected GAI usage that does not comply with this policy, they should speak with their direct supervisor.

title: Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy in the Chicago Public Media Newsroom
paragraph: An internal AI Oversight Committee has been established and meets quarterly or as required to: Create and update an AI policy Review new AI tools and use cases Assess potential risks or ethical concerns Update policy and standards as needed

title: Protos Security Marks 20 Years with Launch of AI-Driven Security Operations Platform at ISC West 2026
paragraph: LAS VEGAS, March 3, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As security threats grow more complex and organizations demand greater transparency and accountability from their providers, Protos Security will debut a new AI-powered operational agent at ISC West 2026, designed to bring real-time insight and automation directly into guarding and off-duty programs. title: Protos Security Marks 20 Years with Launch of AI-Driven Security Operations Platform at ISC West 2026
paragraph: Protos currently maintains 98% customer retention and supports its national footprint through a network of more than 2,800 vendor branch locations and 60,000 off-duty law enforcement professionals.
