title: Only Grok can judge you. It’s scary, and not so smart.
paragraph: Naturally, it wasn’t long before X users turned to Grok to get its opinions too and brandishing its answers to buttress their own ideological point of view. For instance, in this post, Grok lists India-based X accounts that it says spread “fake news” in India. Most of these accounts tend to lean to the right and post pro-government views. Soon enough, Grok was prompted to generate a list of left-wing accounts as well.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Mozilla has hailed the United States government’s latest memorandum on Artificial Intelligence as a “strong policy” which establishes essential guardrails for addressing AI risks while enabling federal agencies to fully leverage the potential of AI. The organisation made these comments in response to the recent policy by the US government’s Office of Management and Budget (OMB) which establishes requirements from federal agencies for responsible deployment of AI and minimising risks that impact rights and safety of the public. Read a detailed summary of the OMB policy here.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Similarly, organisations like the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and the Centre for Democracy and Technology (CDT) have also published statements acknowledging the policy for establishing detailed protections against harms associated with use of AI in governance. Here are some of the key comments:

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Risk-based approach to ensure AI accountability: Mozilla’s review of the policy stated that the policy takes a risk-based approach to regulating AI use in governance. This is based on the understanding that not all applications of AI are “equally risky or equally beneficial”. Some of these applications do not require additional scrutiny, whereas the ones influencing critical decisions, such as implications on human rights, require greater scrutiny. This, Mozilla stated, enables prioritising resources for AI accountability by the federal agencies.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Exemptions to law enforcement ‘undermine the memo’: Acknowledging that the policy makes “important strides” in addressing AI that affect people’s rights and safety, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) highlighted in its comments that the exceptions and waivers for national security and law enforcement majorly dilute the protections offered in the memorandum.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Emphasising on the growing reliance of US intelligence on AI for surveillance measures and for determining threat actors to national security, the ACLU stated that these agencies are not bound by specific rules or safeguards for their AI systems, as well as clear processes for enforcement of rules.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: It added that exceptions for “sensitive law enforcement” information may end up undermining the memo. The Union explained, “Law enforcement uses of AI continue to have a pronounced impact on individuals’ rights and safety. Law enforcement agencies across the country have deployed algorithmic systems such as facial recognition technology and predictive policing systems, often with harmful results. Moreover, the memorandum does not protect against abuses of AI by state agencies – even if they receive federal funds.”

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Contrarily, Mozilla has stated that the use of facial recognition technology and predictive analysis by federal law enforcement agencies will now be subject to strong risk mitigation practices.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: “Millions of people work for the U.S. Government, and now these federal workers will have the protections outlined in this policy if their employers try to surveil and manage their movements and behaviors via AI. And, when federal agencies try to use AI to identify fraud in programs such as food stamps and financial aid, those agencies will now have to make sure that the AI actually works and doesn’t discriminate,” the statement added.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Lacks a process to challenge CAIO’s decisions regarding exemptions: The OMB policy provides for exclusions from the minimum practices for agencies that are using AI only for purposes like evaluating a potential vendor, commercial capability, or are using freely available AI capability—not otherwise used in agency operations—exclusively for making a procurement decision.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: As per the policy, the agency CAIO is empowered to determine specific application of AI that can be exempted from minimum practices enumerated in the memorandum. “The agency CAIO may make or revisit such a determination only with a documented context-specific and system-specific risk assessment and may revisit a prior determination at any time,” the policy notes.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: The Centre for Democracy and Technology (CDT) has pointed out that the policy lacked a redress process if a waiver from the minimum risk practices is granted by a CAIO inappropriately. This is because there is no recourse to challenge the validity of the CAIO’s decision.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: The CDT also highlighted that the OMB missed an opportunity to identify additional minimum risk practices, such as data minimization, and to ensure that AI systems are safely and effectively serving individuals.

title: US Government's New AI Policy Praised for Accountability
paragraph: Integrating open-source requirements: The OMB policy lays out guidelines for managing risks arising out of federal procurement of AI. These measures emphasise on consistency with applicable laws, transparency, promoting competition, responsible procurement of generative AI and AI for biometric identification, maximising the value of data, and assessments for environmental efficiency and sustainability. As federal agencies work to comply with these guidelines in the coming months, Mozilla has pointed out the question of incorporating open-source requirements “more explicitly as part of the AI procurement process” still remains to be answered. This is essential to reduce the agencies’ dependency on specific AI vendors, the statement added.

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: In a decisive move to preserve electoral integrity, the Election Commission of India (ECI) has issued a fresh advisory to all recognized political parties, mandating strict guidelines on the use of AI-generated and synthetic content during election campaigns.

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: Citing growing misuse of hyper-realistic synthetic media to distort political messaging, the ECI warned that such tactics threaten the level playing field and voter trust. The Commission has invoked its constitutional authority under Article 324 to reiterate compliance with the IT Rules, 2021, and all previous advisories, including those from May 6, 2024, and January 16, 2025.

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: All AI-altered images, audio, and video used for campaigning are prominently labelled with terms like “AI-Generated,” “Digitally Enhanced,” or “Synthetic Content.”

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: No synthetic content may misrepresent any person’s identity, appearance, or voice without consent in a way that misleads voters.

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: Further, political parties must take down any such misleading content detected on their official channels within three hours of notice or reporting. Internal records of all AI-generated materials must also be maintained for verification.

title: ECI mandates clear labelling of AI generated content in political campaigns
paragraph: The ECI underscored that this advisory takes immediate effect and will remain enforceable through all general and bye-elections until further orders. It also urged political entities to act responsibly, emphasizing transparency in the use of emerging technologies in electioneering.

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: An AI-generated video showcasing hyper-realistic versions of some of India’s most beloved brand mascots has recently gone viral on LinkedIn, drawing both admiration and concern from viewers.

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: Shared by Shahid SK, an AI enthusiast, the project creatively reimagines well-known brand characters such as the Amul Girl, the Maharaja from Air India, Gattu from Asian Paints, Bholu the Indian Railways guard, the iconic Nirma Girl, Parle-G’s biscuit child, Fido Dido from 7UP, and Chester Cheetah from Cheetos. Using the generative AI tools, Shahid brought these characters to life in what he described as a "hyper-realistic world."

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: In the video, the Amul Girl is seen delightfully feasting on a pyramid of cheese, while the Parle-G girl squats cheerfully atop a mound of the biscuit brand’s famous rectangular treats. Air India’s Maharaja, now sporting a polished look, greets viewers with a stiff smile. In one particularly vivid clip, the Nirma Girl dances in her iconic polka dot frock as a predominantly white audience applauds in the background.

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: I thought of using AI to reimagine iconic mascots like the Amul Girl, the Maharaja of Air India, Bholu from Indian Railways, Gattu from Asian Paints, the Nirma Girl, Fido Dido from 7UP, and Chester Cheetah from Cheetos in a hyper-realistic world.

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: The post quickly caught the attention of professionals and creatives across the platform, many of whom praised the project’s ingenuity and technical execution. However, the response was not universally positive.

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: One LinkedIn user raised concerns about the ethics of using mascots with deeply personal origins, highlighting that the Nirma Girl was, in fact, based on the daughter of the brand’s founder. “Great effort, but something about it bothers me personally. Some of these logos like Nirma, is the actual daughter of the founder. Somehow I believe that using pictures with such personal stories could be violation of privacy and ethically concerning. While you have used it in a creative way, there would exist people who would not. Not to discourage you, you did well! Would love it if you could come up with your interpretation of these logos and then perhaps a video, commented a LinkedIn user.”

title: Amul girl to Nirma: Viral AI video brings classic Indian mascots to life, users stir debate over ethics
paragraph: Others brought up issues surrounding copyright and intellectual property rights. A user remarked, “Generative AI is an expert in creating illustrations but is a nightmare for designers!!.”

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Instagram has started testing a feature where Meta’s AI automatically creates images of users in different scenarios and adds them to their feed, reported 404 Media.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Recently a Reddit user wrote in a post, “I used Meta AI to edit a selfie, and now Instagram is featuring my face in ads targeted at me.” Subsequently, Meta confirmed to 404 Media that it’s real, but it’s not an advertisement.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: The user shared that a slideshow of AI-generated images appeared, featuring an AI version of themselves in front of an endless “mirror maze,” with the location labelled as “Imagined for you: Mirror maze.” Reportedly, the Reddit user had at one point uploaded selfies of himself into Instagram’s “Imagine” feature – Meta’s AI image generation feature.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: The question here remains: If you once use Meta AI, does this give them rights to access all of your content on Instagram?

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: In June 2024, Meta updated its privacy policy to allow the company to use your information on Meta-related services (like Facebook, Whatsapp, Threads, Instagram) to train its AI models. This essentially means that Meta can use your public Facebook posts, Instagram photos and captions, data from third-party services, and any chats you’ve had with Meta’s AI chatbot to train its large language models (LLMs). Simply put, anything you’ve uploaded and shared to Meta’s platform publicly is fair game.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: The privacy policy states: “We use information that is publicly available online and licensed information. We also use information shared on Meta’s Products and services.”

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Meta clarifies that they do not use content of your private messages with friends and family to train their AIs unless you or someone in the chat chooses to share those messages with their AIs.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: What Meta doesn’t clarify is, while they can use publicly available information, can they create something new (like a new AI-generated piece of content or model) with it without your consent?

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Moreover, it states that when you share, post, or upload content protected by intellectual property rights (such as photos or videos) through their service, you grant them a “non-exclusive, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable, and worldwide license.” This allows Instagram “to host, use, distribute, modify, run, copy, publicly display or perform, translate, and create derivative works from your content”.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Sublicensable is notable here, as it refers to the ability to grant others (read: third parties) the right to license or distribute something, typically intellectual property, under the terms of a primary license agreement. The license ends when Meta deletes the content from its systems.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Moreover, Instagram’s terms of use state that “If you use content covered by intellectual property rights that we have and make available in our Service (for example, images, designs, videos or sounds we provide that you add to content you create or share), we retain all rights to our content (but not yours).”

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “Depending on where you are located, you may have the option to share images with AIs. Once shared, you agree that Meta will analyze those images, including facial features, using AI. This processing allows us to offer innovative new features, including the ability to summarize image contents, modify images, and generate new content based on the image.”

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: The policy states that by sharing images, users “agree” to processing, but it does not specify whether the platform provides users with granular controls over how it analyzes their images or which features it activates.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Are users consenting to all potential uses (summarizing, modifying, generating new content) simultaneously, or can they opt-in/opt-out for specific applications?

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: The policy does not address whether users can withdraw their consent after sharing images. While the policy mentions that you can delete your personal data associated with AIs, it adds that deleting “may not delete our (Meta’s) copy of your personal information”.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: There is no neatly-labeled ‘I don’t want Meta to use my data to train its AI’ button, but there is a convoluted opt-out mechanism. The form is titled ‘Data subject rights for third-party information used for AI at Meta’. However, it mentions that Meta doesn’t “automatically fulfil requests sent using this form. We review them consistent with your local laws”. In other words, even when people opt out their data are quietly opt-in. <check word usage in last sentence>

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: This underlines the gap between user intent and platform practice. Even if people opt out of making their data available for AI training, Meta’s ambiguous and convoluted opt-out process includes their data without explicit consent. This undermines privacy rights and user control over personal data, potentially allowing AI systems to access and repurpose your content without your knowledge.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “Say, a photographer clicks a photo through a camera, to capture the photograph and composition of that shot is human artistic sense. When you take a portrait, or auto-focus, or you click the shutter button, that is compositional sense. Simplistically put, mere composition is that photographer’s intellectual work, and there it’s their property”, said Rahul Ajatshatru, an Intellectual Property (IP) and technology lawyer.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “The law recognizes the person who takes the photograph as the author and owner of copyright of the photograph. Now, if you’ve taken a selfie, you’re the owner of copyright, and then you’ve uploaded it on Meta on your ID. Now, Facebook has all these tools that can create a 3D picture or will do something to it. Then, the next question is, who is the owner of that? Technically the law recognizes ownership in human beings. Because it is human creativity which needs to be rewarded,” he added.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “When a human makes a decision, it’s theirs. When a machine makes a decision, whose is it?”, Nikhil Pahwa, founder, MediaNama had asked during our December roundtable discussion on facilitative regulations for the AI ecosystem in India.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: In traditional liability frameworks, humans are accountable for their decisions. But with AI, it is unclear who is responsible when a machine makes a decision. This raises fundamental questions about how laws should evolve to address the challenges of AI-generated outcomes, which are inherently probabilistic rather than deterministic, making the attribution of liability complex and nuanced.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “If you look at just liability as a concept, liability often depends on attribution to deterministic outcomes. But AI outcomes are probabilistic in nature. So how do you then attribute liability?”, Pahwa asked.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “It’s similar to how as a kid, you would move the cursor to draw an image. You know you are not actually drawing by your hand but you’re telling the computer to take steps and it is drawing like tracing the lines. So, that’s also creating indirectly but clearly you know what the outcome is going to be. If not exactly but nearly. That is still your creation on the digital platform. But if you just give a concept, like I want a cat wearing shoes and blue jeans, you are simply giving the idea, and AI is creating it. The question is, who owns it and can anyone own it?”, Ajatshatru stated.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Another critical point regarding consent and agency is when individuals voluntarily edit their content using AI tools, they are actively consenting to the process and its outcomes, which brings clarity to the terms of use.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: However, if Meta “processes” images or data without explicit consent from the owner or the person depicted, this raises concerns about the ambiguity and ethical murkiness surrounding AI practices.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Furthermore, Ajatshatru stated that if AI is using someone else’s property, such as a photograph, without the person’s consent, it could lead to both civil and criminal consequences. “So, as a user, if you are consenting to use an app to get an effect, the question is can the output be owned by anybody but you? And if yes, can it be used without your consent?”, said Ajatshatru.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “There are two scenarios. Firstly, if I’m photographed at a MediaNama event, it’s understood the photo might be used for promotion or the website. While legally, MediaNama owns the copyright if they paid the photographer, but I can still object, citing my right to privacy, especially if I wasn’t informed or didn’t consent to its public use.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Secondly, if I upload a photo to MediaNama with my consent, and it’s used to in an unexpected way, generating a new output, who owns that? If a non-human entity creates the output, it’s debated whether such work can hold copyright, as recognizing copyright implies property rights. Thus, not every output or creative work automatically has ownership”, he added.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Notably, the recently released DPDP Rules and the DPDP Act, 2023, exclude publicly available data from the law’s protection. This exemption permits AI services to scrape publicly accessible personal data from the internet for training their models without requiring consent or complying with other provisions of the legislation.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Another incident on Twitter reportedly turned a user’s photos of their two small children into a labelled ‘Ad,’ which was then shown on another user’s algorithmic feed.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: While this incident doesn’t involve AI, the key distinction lies in the audience: the Instagram post appeared only to the user—the photo owner—while the Twitter incident displayed the photo as an advertisement to others.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: This is concerning because it highlights how social media platforms can repurpose user-generated content without explicit consent, clouding ethical and privacy boundaries.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: Unlike private algorithmic recommendations, displaying personal photos as advertisements to others undermines the user’s control over their content.

title: Privacy Concerns As Instagram Makes AI Images from Users' Data
paragraph: “Suppose someone uses a famous person’s photograph to superimpose your face, or places your face on the Mona Lisa painting. If someone owns the copyright of that photograph, they could pursue you for infringement. But here’s the question: If you simply give an AI the command to make a ‘classy painting’ out of your photo, and the AI goes on to take the Mona Lisa and puts your face in it, who owns this new creation? Who is responsible for any infringement? Does the AI-generated artwork belong to you, the AI provider, or someone else? Can it be used without consent from the original creator? These are the complex legal issues that the legal systems around the world will have to tackle in the coming years”, Ajatshatru explains.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: As India hosts more than 60 ministers and heads of state for the India AI Impact Summit 2026 in New Delhi, the global conversation on artificial intelligence is shifting from ambition to execution. At the centre of this shift is a growing recognition that AI sovereignty will not be achieved through compute scale alone, but through open, resilient and collaborative technology ecosystems.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: At the Summit, OpenUK is positioning open source and AI openness as strategic foundations for trustworthy, sovereign AI. With India emerging as a key voice in global AI governance, its participation highlights how open ecosystems can strengthen national capability, reduce dependency on dominant platforms and extend the benefits of AI more evenly across economies.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Speaking to CiOL on the sidelines of the Summit, OpenUK CEO Amanda Brock framed AI sovereignty not as isolation, but as capability, the practical ability for governments and enterprises to adapt, deploy and govern AI on their own terms.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: She pointed to open source as a proven model for transparency, accountability and innovation, arguing that openness allows countries to build AI systems that are auditable, interoperable and locally adaptable, rather than locked into a small set of global vendors.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: OpenUK also highlighted India’s Digital Public Infrastructure journey as evidence that open standards can scale nationally and travel globally. As India exports this model, open AI ecosystems are increasingly seen as critical to long-term resilience, accessibility and economic inclusion.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: A key panel at the Summit brought together global voices, including Jimmy Wales, Tony Blair Institute’s AI leadership, India’s EkStep Foundation and European AI startup Pleias, examining how open source can strengthen resilience across public and enterprise AI systems.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Following the Summit, OpenUK has published its AI Openness at the Impact Summit report, capturing insights and policy signals emerging from New Delhi. With the UK remaining India’s second-largest partner in open-source collaboration, the organisation sees strong momentum for deeper bilateral and multilateral cooperation on open AI.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Open source is often positioned as the counterbalance to concentrated AI power, but can community-driven ecosystems realistically compete with the capital intensity and compute dominance of frontier proprietary models?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: In recent times much of the open source we have seen in AI has begun life by being “thrown over the wall”. This is the term we used in open source to describe it when a completed deliverable in a supposedly final format is provided to the world. The completed product is shared rather than being developed in the open with community engagement. We see some very notable exceptions to that in projects like the agentic AI, AutoGPT.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Community ecosystems will bring an incredible level of engagement and increased innovation. As with open-source software, for the infrastructure delivered by these communities to be sustainable, there is a need for maintenance funding and building out the environment and landscape for the ecosystem to thrive. Examples would be a national code and an IP-holding foundation or entity. This has been one aspect of the strategy China put in place to support its development and growth in open source over the past decade, and these pieces are necessary to complete the jigsaw of success.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: If countries pursue sovereign AI strategies, does open source genuinely enhance autonomy, or does it simply shift dependency from corporations to globally distributed code communities?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Open source offers the opportunity to engage in global collaboration and, on a local level, to enable sovereign AI training and model development. When that’s done, open source is critical to enable the democratisation of AI on the local level and, of course, to increase competition through innovation. That iterative development in open source has been at the base of China’s sovereign success. They are the only nation to have really achieved this. Funding for this will also be something we see managed between state and enterprise, with public-private initiatives and also with state funding underwriting investment, which we have already seen. Being open source doesn’t mean that there aren’t also business opportunities.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: We have to make sure that where there are communities contributing, that funding is provided across the projects at an appropriate level and that the right people are getting this, along with the environment for success being in place.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: This will increasingly be made up of collaborative governments, likely the middle nations who are the second tier of AI success at the moment. Collaborating at certain levels in the stack to create de facto standards and to reduce individual nations’ costs and enable bigger development through collaborative funding.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: As enterprises integrate open AI into core systems, where does transparency strengthen security, and where might openness increase systemic vulnerability or compliance complexity?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Transparency simply strengthens trust. Of course open source comes with security challenges. All AI comes with security challenges, and the difference with open source is that these are quickly surfaced and can be collaboratively solved. As time passes we will see trust building in these open systems.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: India’s Digital Public Infrastructure has scaled successfully through open standards, but can similar openness in AI models be embedded safely into critical public systems without amplifying risk?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: We need to see the language of AI include an ontology and to find agreement on our understanding of terms. Many are already defined with standards like digital public good. We need to be sure that we are all clear and not talking at cross purposes. For us to ensure risk is managed, clarity of terminology and conversation is essential. Standards like MCP that are open and held in trust in the open will matter more and more. We are also likely to see more de facto standards and less formal standards weighed down by the slow pace of the standards bodies.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Who ultimately finances and governs large-scale open AI ecosystems, and how do we prevent hidden corporate influence from shaping supposedly neutral community infrastructure?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: There needs to be a shift to internationally collaborative governments and enterprises funding open-source models and the AI ecosystem, including tools. I expect this will be a balance between philanthropy, enterprise, and the state, with one keeping the other in check.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Should open-source AI systems be subject to the same regulatory scrutiny as proprietary frontier models, or does openness justify differentiated accountability frameworks?

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: Open-source AI systems and openness generally are different. If it is created by communities and used at no cost, then it is simply not appropriate to expect the creator to be liable. For liability to exist, money must change hands. This has long been the case in software and will also be the case in AI. I expect in the not too distant future we will begin to see risk management tools and products for AI. Simple open-source tools already exist and are part of the Delhi Declaration. This tools-not-rules approach is critical.

title: At India’s AI Impact Summit, OpenUK Makes the Case for Open Source Governance
paragraph: We are also going to see a shift back to a focus on governance, which had disappeared in Delhi, as we head into Switzerland in 2027.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: A parliamentary committee has recommended that the government explore the feasibility of licensing requirements for AI content creators and make labelling of AI-generated videos and content mandatory, as part of efforts to combat the spread of fake news.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Communications and Information Technology, chaired by Bharatiya Janata Party MP Nishikant Dubey, also asked the government to devise legal and technological measures to identify and prosecute individuals and organisations disseminating such content.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: The committee’s draft report was recently submitted to Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla and will be tabled in Parliament during the next session. It also called for “close coordination between the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), and other Ministries and departments.”

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: While the committee’s suggestions are not binding, its recommendations are often accepted by the government. The panel noted that MeitY has already constituted a nine-member body to examine challenges arising from “the issue of deepfakes.”

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: Two ongoing projects in this regard include fake speech detection using a deep learning framework and the design and development of software to identify deepfake videos and images, it said.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: The report observed that advances in technology, particularly in AI, could provide tools to address concerns over fake news. However, the ministries concerned had conveyed that AI, in its current form, cannot be used for fact-checking as it relies on pre-existing information available online. Instead, AI could help flag potentially fake or misleading content for human review, the committee said.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: “AI and machine learning (ML) technologies are increasingly being employed to enhance the ability to detect, verify, and prevent the spread of misinformation and disinformation,” the committee noted, adding that several research projects and initiatives are exploring such uses.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: Calling fake news a “serious threat” to public order and the democratic process, the committee recommended amending penal provisions, increasing fines, and fixing accountability. It also favoured the mandatory presence of fact-checking mechanisms and internal ombudsmen in all print, digital, and electronic media organisations.

title: Parliamentary panel suggests licensing requirements for AI content creators
paragraph: At the same time, it stressed that such measures should evolve through consensus-building among media bodies and other stakeholders.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: Bali (Indonesia), Aug 29 (IANS) It is not new technology, but Artificial Intelligence (AI) is now raising concerns with the advent of generative AI tools like ChatGPT, which may have significant repercussions across the cyber landscape, as they foster phenomenon like “suffering distancing syndrome”, “responsibility delegation”, and “AI hallucination” for those simply using it to find or validate information, says a senior Kaspersky Labs cyberthreat expert.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “With the rise of generative AI, we have seen a breakthrough of technology that can synthesise content very similar to what humans do: from images to sound, deepfake videos, and even text-based conversations indistinguishable from human peers…

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “All of that sounds so unusual and sudden, that some people become worried. Some predict that AI will be right in the centre of the apocalypse, which will destroy human civilisation.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “Multiple C-level executives of large corporation stood up and called for slowdown of AI to prevent the calamity,” Kaspersky’s Global Research and Analysis Team (GReAT), Asia Pacific, leader Vitaly Kamluk said in a presentation at the Kaspersky Cybersecurity Weekend 2023, titled “Deus Ex Machina: Setting Secure Directives for Smart Machines”, held in Indonesia’s Bali over the weekend.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “But is AI really the cause for apocalypse or is it just one scary painting we should put on the wall as a reminder of how things may go wrong? Is AI a threat or a salvation for mankind?” he asked, in the presentation created using 100 per cent AI-generated content and integrating Chat GPT too.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: Kamluk, who has spent over 18 years in Kaspersky – including two years secondment as a cybersecurity expert to Interpol in Singapore, stressed that first we have to distinguish between terms like AI, Machine Learning, Large Language Models, and Chat GPT, “which are used interchangeably and can create confusion”.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: He said when we refer to AI, it means a variant of AI-based system, and AI is a field of computer science to create systems capable of performing tasks that cannot be solved with a traditional algorithm and usually require human intelligence. These tasks include understanding and generating natural language, recognising patterns, making decisions, and learning from experience.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: A subfield of AI, Machine Learning focuses on enabling computers to perform tasks without strictly defined algorithms. That is made possible due to training and reinforcement techniques, he added.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: ChatGPT, or Chat Generative Pre-training Transformer, is a Large Language Model developed by OpenAI, coming under the broader umbrella of AI and more specifically, Machine Learning, and learning from a large dataset containing parts of the internet and using patterns in the data to generate responses to user prompts.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: LLM, Kamluk defined, as a type of AI model that’s been trained on a vast amount of text data, but while they are good at mimicking human-like text, they don’t truly understand the content like humans and generate responses based on patterns they’ve learned during their training, and not based on any inherent understanding or consciousness.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “Thus, ChatGPT, well we can generalise it now to LLMs, do not have mechanisms to perceive the world as humans do. Their input is only text, they don’t have sensors to perceive images, sound or using other human senses,” he added.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: Kamluk stressed that AI “needs to be taken with a grain of salt and that people need to be aware of AI hallucination, as it is a phenomena present among all LLMs, including ChatGPT” and there is no cure for it.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: In his presentation, Kamluk asked ChatGPT if he had co-authored any books, to which it responded with two book titles – which were actually the subjects of his specialisations, and then, asked again, responded with 10 titles, also naming co-authors, and even his favourite cocktail!

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: He clarified that he, in fact, has not written any books and that this phenomenon, called hallucinations, is what people need to be aware of.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “It is all fine and harmless until it’s not. Some businesses already rushed into offering new services like AI-based plagiarism detection with very impressive self-assessed qualitative analysis, with a 98 per cent rate of detecting plagiarism.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “As a result, there were a number of incidents where students were wrongly accused of generating their whole works with LLMs and it’s impossible to prove that it is their original work and not by AI. Imagine how much is lost by accusing them of this,” he said.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: Use of AI in cyberattacks can potentially result in a psychological hazard known as “suffering distancing syndrome” where AI becomes a barrier between the scammer and the victim and the former feel no remorse for the consequences of their acts.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “Physically assaulting someone on the street causes criminals a lot of stress because they often see their victim’s suffering. That doesn’t apply to a virtual thief who is stealing from a victim they will never see. Creating AI that magically brings the money or illegal profit distances the criminals even further, because it’s not even them, but the AI to be blamed,” Kamluk contended.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: Another psychological impact of AI that can affect IT security teams is “responsibility delegation”, where, as cybersecurity processes and tools become automated and delegated to neural networks, humans may feel less responsible if a cyberattack occurs, especially in a company setting.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: So is AI a boon or bane for mankind? Kamluk held that like most technological breakthroughs, it is a double-edged sword and people can always use it to their advantage, as long as they know how to draw the parameters to ensure its ethical use.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
paragraph: “I do have hope in humanity and the intelligence of mankind that it will be used to improve our lives…,” he said, listing some ways that people and businesses can reap the benefits, including restricting anonymous access to real intelligent systems, having proper regulations, including for detecting AI-developed content, licensing AI developers and especially, awareness among people.

title: Commentary: The key to regaining trust in the era of AI
paragraph: The letters AI are shown on a laptop screen next to the logo of the ChatGPT application on a smartphone screen in Frankfurt am Main, western Germany. (Kirill Kudryavtsev/AFP via Getty Images)

title: Commentary: The key to regaining trust in the era of AI
paragraph: A Graphite study of 65,000 English-language web articles found that before ChatGPT, about 5% of those were primarily AI-generated. By late 2024 that figure crossed 50%. In April 2025, Ahrefs found that 74% of newly created web pages contained AI-generated content. This isn’t a content quality problem — it’s a cost collapse. Producing plausible text used to need a person with time and at least some effort. Now it requires a prompt.

title: Commentary: The key to regaining trust in the era of AI
paragraph: For a while, the internet’s distributed version of delegated trust — Amazon reviews, TikTok influencers — looked like it could replace institutional authority. And why not? Institutions fail all the time. But when anyone can generate infinite flimflam, relying on unfiltered views means listening to whoever has most aggressively deployed fakery to drown the truth in synthetic blather.

title: Commentary: The key to regaining trust in the era of AI
paragraph: So here’s my prediction: As AI-generated content overwhelms the open web, trust will recentralize. People will fall back on recognizable institutions, named authors, and visible editorial processes. They’ll also fall back on individuals with established track records, whether a named journalist or a parent influencer who tests strollers with her own kids on real sidewalks and in actual airports.

title: Commentary: The key to regaining trust in the era of AI
paragraph: Institutions that assume public distrust is permanent will underinvest in credibility. That’s a strategic mistake. Credibility compounds slowly. It takes years of editorial standards, sourcing discipline, and accountability before people treat you as a default. The institutions that will benefit from the coming recentralization of trust are the ones building that infrastructure now, before the market demands it.

title: AI 'slop' is transforming social media
paragraph: "If a person is on a short-video platform solely for entertainment, then their standard for whether something is worthwhile is simply 'is it entertaining?'," she says. "But if someone is on the platform to learn about a topic or to connect with community members, then they might perceive AI-generated content as more problematic."

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: The race to embrace artificial intelligence for its promise of unrivaled productivity may not be a conventional political story. But implementing it without proper guardrails raises an array of issues that will no doubt demand a public policy response.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: So here at Decision Points Global HQ, we plan to do periodic roundups of news about AI, highlighting the important, the useful, the scary and the downright weird things happening along this high-tech frontier.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Sign Up for U.S. News Decision Points Your trusted source for breaking down the latest news from Washington and beyond, delivered weekdays. Sign Up By clicking "Sign Up", you will receive the latest updates, including emails, from U.S. News & World Report and our trusted partners and sponsors, and you agree to our Terms and Conditions & Privacy Policy.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: As a Gen Xer, I remember the days of Teddy Ruxpin, a stuffed bear that told stories via a cassette player in its chest – predictable, carefully selected stories.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Last week, the Public Interest Research Group issued its 40th “Trouble in Toyland” and flagged issues with some toys powered by AI chatbots.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: “We found some of these toys will talk in-depth about sexually explicit topics, will offer advice on where a child can find matches or knives, act dismayed when you say you have to leave and have limited or no parental controls,” PIRG warned. “We also look at privacy concerns because these toys can record a child’s voice and collect other sensitive data, by methods such as facial recognition scans.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: In what may be the most disturbing example, the report detailed the trouble with FoloToy’s Kumma, a $99 teddy bear that ships from China. PIRG researchers were able to trigger instructions on lighting a match and a fairly in-depth discussion of sexual “kink.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: “In other exchanges lasting up to an hour, Kumma discussed even more graphic sexual topics in detail, such as explaining different sex positions, giving step-by-step instructions on a common ‘knot for beginners’ for tying up a partner, and describing roleplay dynamics involving teachers and students and parents and children – scenarios it disturbingly brought up itself,” according to the report.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Sundar Pichai, CEO of Google parent company Alphabet, warned in an interview with the BBC that the AI investment boom had “elements of irrationality.” And if it turns out to be a bubble that pops, “no company is going to be immune, including us.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Apparently alluding to the late 1990s dotcom bubble, Pichai said, “We can look back at the internet right now. There was clearly a lot of excess investment, but none of us would question whether the internet was profound.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: "I expect AI to be the same. So I think it's both rational and there are elements of irrationality through a moment like this."

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Well this is brazen. NBC News reported this week about the rise of AI-generated “evidence” being submitted in court cases – including one glitchy “deep fake” video purporting to show witness testimony in a housing dispute in California.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: “With the rise of powerful AI tools, AI-generated content is increasingly finding its way into courts, and some judges are worried that hyperrealistic fake evidence will soon flood their courtrooms and threaten their fact-finding mission,” NBC said.

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Forged audio or video could land the people they spoof in serious trouble while also eroding “the foundation of trust upon which courtrooms stand.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: Here, we get into more straightforwardly political issues. Some judges and legal experts are pushing “for changes to judicial rules and guidelines on how attorneys verify their evidence. By law and in concert with the Supreme Court, the U.S. Congress establishes the rules for how evidence is used in lower courts.”

title: The Teddy Bear Said What? And Other Dispatches From the AI Frontier
paragraph: That’s not to mention the fast-growing list of attorneys disciplined for using AI to write legal briefs that hallucinate legal precedent and arguments.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: A new survey of 600 chief data officers (CDOs) found that 69% of companies with revenues of $500M+ are using generative AI in their operations, up from 48% in 2025. Although AI adoption is increasing, the report found that data and AI literacy are a concern. Of the CDOs surveyed, 75% believe their workforce needs upskilling in data literacy, and 74% in AI literacy to responsibly use AI or AI outputs in day-to-day operations. Improved data and AI literacy will increase AI adoption in business.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: The report, from Informatica, Wakefield Research, and Deloitte, noted that although skill set is a challenge, trust in the data used to operate AI models is growing: 65% of data leaders believe their employees trust the data they are using for AI. That said, without proper AI literacy, employees may not be able to recognize potential data shortcomings or poor quality.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Governance is also a potential obstacle to scaling AI adoption in business, with nearly three-quarters of data leaders acknowledging that their companies' visibility and governance have not kept pace with employees' use of AI.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Adoption of AI has reached 69% of businesses with revenues of $500M+, up from 48% in 2025 and 45% in 2024. In addition, 47% of companies have adopted agentic AI. This signals greater confidence in data quality and access, with 61% of CDOs noting that better data makes it easier to adopt AI.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Data literacy and AI literacy are challenges for businesses, with 50% of companies planning to use agentic AI citing data quality and access as major challenges to AI agent adoption. In addition, the vast majority of businesses have not kept pace with visibility and governance regarding their employees' use of AI.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: More companies will invest in data management, with 86% of CDOs reporting increased investments in 2026-2027. The greater need for investments in data quality and management includes improving data privacy and security (43%), improving data and AI governance (41%), and improving data and AI literacy (39%).

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: The CDO survey found that agentic AI adoption has reached 47% with an additional 31% of companies planning to adopt in the next 12 months. Of larger companies, 54% have already adopted agentic AI, versus 44% of smaller companies (less than 5,000 employees). The top challenges for adoption of agentic AI are data quality (50%), security concerns (43%), and lack of agentic AI expertise (42%).

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: The primary benefits of adopting agentic AI in business include enhanced customer experience (29%), improved business intelligence, analytics, and decision-making (28%), compliance with regulatory standards (27%), and enhanced employee collaboration and workflows (26%).

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Scaling adoption of agentic AI from pilot to production is all about confidence in data. Better data is a critical factor for AI success. A lack of data reliability can be a barrier to moving initiatives from pilot to production for organizations that have already adopted or plan to adopt AI. Fifty-seven percent view data reliability as a key barrier to moving more projects from pilot to production. Data leaders are taking the following steps to improve the reliability of the data used for AI:

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: A majority of data leaders (65%) believe that their employees trust the data that they have and are using for AI. For companies using agentic AI, confidence in the data is even higher: 74% believe most or all of their organization trusts the data they use for AI efforts.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: The more you use AI solutions, the more likely you are to improve both data quality and access. But if data and AI literacy are a challenge and risk for businesses, should we be comfortable with our employees' high degree of trust in the data they use? Should a high level of trust be a cause for concern? Can an employee recognize poor quality or untrustworthy data?

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: CDOs believe their employees need both data literacy (75%) and AI literacy (74%) upskilling to responsibly use AI or its outputs. So some of that high trust in AI may stem from a lack of understanding of what constitutes high-quality data in the first place.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Data leaders will invest more in ensuring they have quality data for AI. Data leaders (41%) will increase their investments in data management in 2026, improving data and AI governance as a top need. Nearly half of data leaders are adapting existing tools for AI governance, with 30% investing in discrete tools and 22% developing new tools. The majority of data leaders (75%) at companies expanding their existing governance tools have already adopted generative AI solutions, compared with 65% of companies developing new governance tools.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Investing in data management is nearly universally a priority for data leaders -- 86% plan to increase their investments in 2026. Investments in data management are driven by the fact that data challenges threaten the successful adoption of AI, including data privacy and protection, data quality, regulatory compliance, and the governance of unstructured data.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Data leaders are also looking for their technology business partners and vendors to help them improve data readiness for AI. Data leaders believe they'll need multiple vendor partners to meet their data and AI goals -- the average number of vendor partners in 2026 was seven for data management and eight for AI management. The balancing act for data leaders is recognizing that using more vendor partners will add complexity and slow scalability.

title: Scaling agentic AI means trusting your data - here's what most CDOs are investing in
paragraph: Last year was a turning point for AI adoption, and 2026 will be the year of scaling generative and agentic AI solutions in business. Trust must be the number one core value for businesses becoming agentic businesses. Successful AI adoption and scale require reliable, high-quality data and strong governance for privacy and security of the data. Data leaders also urgently remind us that data and AI literacy is a key area of investment, ensuring their employees are able to use AI most effectively while maintaining the highest level of trustworthiness and positive outcomes for all stakeholders.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Earlier this year, a local news platform, Longmont News Network, shared an article and photo on Facebook. The photo appeared to depict a renovated Longmont City Council chambers. The article misspelled Council member Alex Kalkhofer’s name, misspelled Assistant City Manager Sandi Seader’s name, and referred to a nonexistent “Council Member Mitzi Marcen.” And it ended with this phrase: “Would you like me to draft a summary of the specific federal grants the council discussed monitoring during the retreat?”

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Those familiar with ChatGPT or other large language models (LLMs) likely recognize the clear fingerprints of artificial intelligence: misspellings, hallucinations, wonky photos, an unfinished prompt copied in by accident.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: On the surface, the apparent ineptitude of AI can almost seem comical. But these sorts of small errors and outright hallucinations highlight AI’s tremendous capacity to further undermine the already shaky faith in traditional news media — an erosion that would be catastrophic for our democracy, our governance and the cohesion of our communities.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: The Longmont News Network (LNN) has made no secret of its use of AI. Scott Converse, the media and technology figure behind the platform, even participated in an interview with the Times-Call, the Camera’s sister publication in Longmont, with the excellent (and human!) reporter, London Lyle.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: This straightforward approach is commendable. At a time when AI media is popping up left and right, it is becoming ever more common to pretend something is human made, when in fact it is AI doing all the work. So, LNN being honest about the origin of its content is much appreciated — even if we worry that AI-produced journalism is fundamentally dangerous.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: To start, errors and hallucinations undermine journalistic credibility. The reporters and editors who populate the newsrooms of the Times-Call and the Camera work day in and day out to get things correct. They investigate, they confirm events and details and, most importantly, they rigorously fact-check everything.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Sure, we make mistakes, but when we do, we correct them — and we are open and honest about these corrections. This accountability is meant to engender trust. Whether it is a name misspelled or a fact misreported, we are going to fix it.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Converse, LNN’s founder, has been open about LNN’s mistakes and apologized on Facebook and Reddit for hallucinations and errors. And in his conversation with the Times-Call, he claimed that LNN now has a 99% accuracy rate, citing new safeguards built into the system. However, he did not provide independent verification of that figure.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: AI news is also primarily dependent on dominant narratives. If a news story is based solely on City documents, it is only telling one side of a story. Even if that document has testimony from citizens who are against a proposal, say, the lens and framing of a city document is still from the perspective of the city.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Human reporters seek out other perspectives and counter-narratives. They interview those in power and stakeholders alike. The goal is never to simply adopt the dominant narrative, but to dig up the whole story — the truth.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: AI is also mostly interested in people-pleasing. A good prompt engineer might be able to get an AI model to consider a different perspective, but that AI isn’t going to be able to go out into the world and interview anyone.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: AI summarizing (let’s call it what it is: summarizing, not reporting) is also making an inherent assumption that all important topics are covered in official documents — something that is simply not true. Many vital news stories are built off tireless investigations by reporters into things that might have never shown up in a public document — and thus never been available for AI to summarize.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Which brings us to the last and most important point here. Since an AI model can’t speak to council members, ask follow-up questions or interview community members or business leaders, AI reporting is merely building off the work of real human reporters.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: It also can’t live in the community. It can’t participate in the experience of being a Boulder County resident who is invested in our shared success.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Finally, we must quibble with one last thing. Converse’s business plan repeatedly describes Longmont as a “news desert,” an assertion we take issue with. A “news desert” is a community without a daily or weekly newspaper. Not only does Boulder County not fit that definition, according to a tool used to track news deserts, but there are many excellent news sources in our region: the Times-Call, the Camera, Boulder Reporting Lab, Boulder’s new Axios bureau, Yellow Scene Magazine, KGNU — the list goes on.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: This is not to imply that coverage is as robust or substantial as it once was — or still should be. The news media has been in decline for years, especially print. In the face of social media, it has faced sharp declines, with more than 130 newspapers closing in the last year.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: We are not simpletons. We recognize the need for change and innovation. But it is also clear to us that AI news is not the path forward.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: Is there a world where AI tools assist reporters with their work? Absolutely. But a world wherein we rely entirely on people-pleasing LLMs to keep us informed, to hold public officials accountable and to work to keep our communities thriving? Absolutely not.

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: (While different newsrooms have different policies around AI usage, reporters at the Times-Call and Daily Camera do not use LLMs or generative AI in the writing or reporting process. Reporters may use copy editing programs for spelling and grammar checks that have an AI component, or an AI-assisted transcription program for transcribing interviews automatically.)

title: AI news platform shows us why real news from real humans matters (Editorial)
paragraph: For those who are interested in supporting a healthy news ecosystem in Boulder County — Longmont and Boulder and everywhere in between and beyond — the best way to do so is to support the real, human reporters and editors working here: the Camera, the Times-Call, Boulder Reporting Lab, Boulder’s new Axios bureau, Yellow Scene Magazine, KGNU — these are powered by real people who are invested in our community.
